serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Andhra fish curry weighs approximately 300 g, typically served alongside steamed rice or flatbread as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Fish Curry (composite estimate); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fish, coconut milk, and cashews
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Andhra Machli Curry' exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN fish curry composites and USDA SR Legacy data for constituent ingredients (white fish fillet, coconut milk, cashews, tomato, onion, spices). The input record's per_100g values (energy 126.67 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 7.33 g, carbohydrate 5 g) are consistent with this ingredient-based estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where detailed data are unavailable from approved sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on fish curry with coconut milk (GI ~35–45 range for low-carbohydrate spiced fish dishes)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Andhra Machli Curry. GI of 38 is estimated by analogy with low-carbohydrate fish-based curries with coconut milk documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021). Carbohydrate content is 5 g/100 g; glycemic load per 300 g serving = (38 × 15 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.7, rounded to 6.3 accounting for fibre adjustment. This is a low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 316.67 mg/100 g (950 mg per 300 g serving) approaches the upper limit for a single meal in a sodium-restricted diet (target <1500–2000 mg/day total).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request a low-salt preparation, limit portion size to 150–200 g, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted fish; the dish's potassium contribution from fish, tomatoes, and onions partially offsets sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5 g/100 g) and estimated low GI (~38) result in a low glycemic load (~6 per 300 g serving), supporting postprandial blood glucose control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 300 g portion; pair with a low-GI carbohydrate such as brown rice or cauliflower rice rather than white rice, and request reduced coconut milk to limit saturated fat intake which can worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.33 g/100 g (10 g per 300 g serving) and sodium at 316.67 mg/100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; fish provides lean protein and some omega-3 fatty acids which are cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced coconut milk and dried coconut to lower saturated fat, and ensure the dish is prepared with minimal added salt; the anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) and fish-derived omega-3 fatty acids offer partial cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric (curcumin) and ginger (gingerols) are evidence-based anti-inflammatory compounds; fish provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids; saturated fat is below the 5 g/100 g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; those with nightshade sensitivity should request removal of tomatoes and green chillies, and coconut milk quantity should be reduced to minimise saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Andhra Fish Curry (also known as Andhra Machli Curry) is a South Indian-inspired fish curry dish served in Singapore restaurants, notably at establishments specialising in North and South Indian cuisine. The dish features white fish fillets — typically dory or a similar mild white fish — simmered in a rich, aromatic gravy made from cashews, dried coconut, coconut milk, tomatoes, onions, tamarind, turmeric, red chilli, ginger, garlic, curry leaves, and mustard seeds. The cooking style is slow simmering in gravy, which allows the fish to absorb the complex spice blend while the cashew and coconut base creates a creamy, slightly nutty texture with a deep orange-red colour. The flavour profile is bold, spicy, tangy from tamarind, and mildly sweet from coconut, with a rich umami depth from the fish. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 127 kcal, 10 g protein, 7.3 g fat (3.3 g saturated), 5 g carbohydrate, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 317 mg sodium. The estimated glycemic index is low (~38), making it suitable for blood glucose management when paired with a low-GI carbohydrate. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, sodium content is borderline for hypertension management at a full 300 g serving, and saturated fat from coconut milk warrants moderation for cardiovascular disease patients. The anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger provide evidence-based benefits for inflammatory conditions. This dish is a culturally significant representation of Andhra Pradesh coastal cuisine adapted for the Singapore dining context.
